data.title: Exchange-Traded Funds Mixed, US Equities Higher After Midday
data.description:
Broad Market Indicators

Broad-market exchange-traded fund IWM was down while IVV was up. Actively traded Invesco QQQ Trust (QQQ) rose 1.8%.

US equity indexes rose in choppy midday trading, while most government bond yields surged and crude oil futures sank to a four-year low as investors weighed China's retaliation against President Donald Trump's trade tariffs amid reports that a bailout of farmers is under consideration.

Energy

IShares US Energy ETF (IYE) and Energy Select Sector SPDR (XLE) both were 0.9% lower.

Technology

Technology Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLK) gained 2.6%; iShares US Technology ETF (IYW) and iShares Expanded Tech Sector ETF (IGM) also advanced.

SPDR S&P Semiconductor (XSD) rose 3.3%, and iShares Semiconductor (SOXX) edged up 3%.

Financial

Financial Select Sector SPDR (XLF) dropped 0.5%. Direxion Daily Financial Bull 3X Shares (FAS) shed 1.6%, and its bearish counterpart, Direxion Daily Financial Bear 3X Shares (FAZ) rose 1.4%.

Commodities

Crude oil fell 4%, and the United States Oil Fund (USO) shed 2.2%. Natural gas rose 1.4%, and the United States Natural Gas Fund (UNG) was 1.8% up.

Gold rose 3.9% on Comex, and SPDR Gold Shares (GLD) gained 3.4%. Silver added 2.3%, and iShares Silver Trust (SLV) was up 1.9%.

Consumer

Consumer Staples Select Sector SPDR (XLP) advanced 0.9%; Vanguard Consumer Staples ETF (VDC) and iShares Dow Jones US Consumer Goods (IYK) also moved higher.

Consumer Discretionary Select Sector SPDR (XLY) was 1.2% higher; VanEck Retail ETF (RTH) rose 0.9%, and SPDR S&P Retail (XRT) added 1.3%.

Health Care

Health Care Select Sector SPDR (XLV) was down 0.7%, and iShares US Healthcare (IYH) and Vanguard Health Care ETF (VHT) were in the red. IShares Biotechnology ETF (IBB) fell 1.4%.

Industrial

Industrial Select Sector SPDR (XLI) added 1.1%. Vanguard Industrials (VIS) and iShares US Industrials (IYJ) were also trending higher.

data.title: Minebeamitsumi To Launch Tender Offer For Shibaura Electronics, Challenging Yageo, Nikkei Reports
data.description:
Nikkei:

  • SHIBAURA IS EXPECTED TO ENDORSE THE BID, AND A JOINT PRESS CONFERENCE BY THE TWO FIRMS IS EXPECTED AS EARLY AS APRIL 10

9 April 2025

THE NORTH AMERICAN INCOME TRUST PLC

('the Company')

Purchase of own ordinary shares

On 9 April 2025, the Company purchased 66,330 shares in the market at a price of 290.58p per share. These shares will be held in treasury.

Following the above purchase, the Company's issued ordinary share capital will continue to be 126,923,569 ordinary shares of 5p each, of which 5,613,733 (4.42% of the issued share capital) are held in treasury and have no voting rights.

Holders of the Company's ordinary shares are entitled to one vote for every share held. As treasury shares have no voting rights, the total number of voting rights in the Company is now 121,309,836. Shareholders should use this figure for notifications in keeping with the provisions of DTR 5.

data.title: Eni Seeks Over $11 Billion Valuation for Plenitude Amid Investor Interest, Reuters Reports
Remy Cointreau CEO to Leave GroupUpdate
data.description:
By Andrea Figueras

Remy Cointreau said Chief Executive Eric Vallat would resign after more than five years at the helm of the French luxury liquor group at a time when Europe's spirits sector reckons with President Trump's tariffs.

The company said Wednesday that Vallat would leave in the summer to dedicate himself to a new professional project.

The maker of Louis XIII cognac said a selection process to replace the CEO was already underway. Marie-Amelie de Leusse, chairwoman of the board of directors, will oversee the transition period.

The announcement comes at a challenging time for European drinks makers amid waning demand after a boom in beverage consumption during the pandemic as well as uncertainty in key markets.

In China, a major market for the industry, authorities implemented provisional tariffs on brandy imports from the EU following Brussels's decision to impose duties on Chinese electric vehicles.

In the U.S., the sector faces President Trump's levies on imported goods from Europe. On Wednesday, EU members approved an initial list of U.S. goods that will be subject to countertariffs, giving the bloc power to impose levies on some products as early as next week. American whiskey has been taken off the list in a move aimed at protecting the bloc's drinks manufacturers.

The decision was welcomed by the U.S.'s Distilled Spirits Council, which noted that the U.S.-EU spirits sectors could go back to zero-for-zero tariffs and untangling distilled spirits products from wider trade disputes.

Remy Cointreau previously said it expected a sales decline at the lower end of its previously guided range between 15% and 18% for the year to the end of March 2025.

Write to Andrea Figueras at andrea.figueras@wsj.com

data.title: REG - Induction Healthcare - Directorate Change
data.description:
RNS Number : 3896E Induction Healthcare Group PLC 09 April 2025  

Induction Healthcare Group PLC

("Induction", the "Company", or the "Group")

Board Changes

Induction Healthcare (AIM: INHC), a leading digital health platform driving transformation of healthcare systems worldwide announces that Andy Williams has submitted his resignation to the Board, stepping down from his position as Non-Executive Director and member of the Audit, Remuneration, and Nomination Committees of the Board with immediate effect.

The Board would like to take this opportunity to thank Andy for his contribution to the Company and wish him well for the future.

ENQUIRIES

Induction Healthcare Group

Christopher Samler, Chair

Paul Tambeau, Chief Executive Officer

+44 (0)7712 194092

+44 (0)7983 104443

Singer Capital Markets (Nominated Adviser and Broker)

+44 (0)20 7496 3000

Philip Davies

Oliver Platts

About Induction - www.inductionhealthcare.com

Induction (AIM: INHC) delivers a suite of software solutions through a single integrated platform that transforms care delivery. Our system-wide applications help healthcare providers and administrators to deliver care at any stage remotely as well as face-to-face - giving the communities they serve greater flexibility, control and ease of access. Purpose-built for integration with leading Electronic Medical Record (EMR) platforms, our products offer immediate stand-alone value that becomes even greater when integrated with pre-existing systems.

Used at scale by national and regional healthcare systems, as well as non-health government services, our applications are relied upon by hundreds of thousands of clinicians and millions of patients across almost every hospital in the British Isles.

data.title: News Highlights: Top Global Markets News of the Day - Wednesday at 1 PM ET
data.description:
China Has Readied a Trade-War Arsenal That Takes Aim at U.S. Companies

Beijing's strategy to hit back at President Trump goes well beyond tariffs, targeting companies who bank on their China ties.

Bond Rout Stirs Broader Fears About Market Strains

Treasurys, normally a haven, have sold off sharply this week.

All eyes turn to Treasury's 10-year auction as buyers prepare to balk

The bond market was in the process of convulsing Wednesday morning in reaction to back-and-forth tariffs between the U.S. and China, and worries now are that potential demand could dry up at the Treasury's $39 billion 10-year note auction in the afternoon.

EU Member States Approve Response to U.S. Steel Tariffs

European Union members approved an initial list of U.S. goods that will be subject to tariffs, giving the bloc power to impose levies on some products as early as next week.

Minneapolis Fed's Kashkari Says Tariffs Raise the Bar for Fed Interest-Rate Moves

The central bank's "first priority must be keeping long-run inflation expectations anchored," Kashkari says.

When Does a Market Dive Hit the Rest of the Economy?

Falling stock prices can filter through to the wider economy, even to those who don't own a penny in stocks.

Trump's Tariffs Put Fed Chair Powell in a 'No-Win Situation'

A trade war ties the Fed's hands by pushing up inflation at the same time that uncertainty is sapping growth.

ECB Has Many Instruments It Can Deploy, Cipollone Says

The European Central Bank has many instruments it can deploy in the event that economic disruption and sharp falls in financial markets threaten to turn into a crisis, a senior official said Wednesday.

Trump Administration Revises Port-Fee Plan to Soften Blow to U.S. Exports

The U.S. Trade Representative's office will base fees largely on vessel capacity, ease charges on ships carrying agricultural exports like soybeans and timber.

The Market Is in a New Mode: 'Sell Everything American'

The simultaneous selloff in stocks, bonds and the dollar is a concerning sign of distress, but the critical indicators aren't flashing red yet.

data.title: News Highlights: Top Company News of the Day - Wednesday at 1 PM ET
data.description:
Walmart Confident About Tariff Resilience Despite Sales Volatility

Walmart reaffirmed its first-quarter sales guidance and widened its range for operating income growth to stay flexible on pricing as tariffs go into effect.

Delta Air Lines Pulls Guidance on Trump Tariff Uncertainty. Why Airline Stocks Are Rising.

Delta Air Lines scraps its full-year guidance Wednesday and warns 'growth has largely stalled' amid global economic uncertainty.

Pharma Stocks Slide Further After Trump Reiterates Tariff Threats on Drugs

Drugmakers have said they're still unclear about whether Trump will impose tariffs on finished products or on active pharmaceutical ingredients.

Prada Deal for Versace at Risk of Collapsing With Market in Turmoil

Prada's tentative deal to acquire Versace from Capri for roughly $1.4 billion could be announced as soon as Thursday if Prada's namesake family sign off.

Formula One's TV Rights Are Up for Grabs, but Media Companies Aren't Racing to Bid

Liberty Media's F1 is seeking $150 million to $180 million a year for the fast-growing sport.

7-Eleven Owner Targets Profit Growth Despite Tariff Uncertainty

Seven & i Holding projected a rise in annual profit despite the uncertainty caused by U.S. tariffs, continuing efforts to stave off a takeover attempt by the Canadian owner of Circle K

America's Booze Makers Cheer Whiskey's European Tariff Dodge

The sector's exemption from levies came after lobbying by Europe's spirits industry in countries such as France, home to an important wine industry and cognac makers like Remy Martin and Hennessy, and Italy, where Milan-based Campari Group owns U.S. bourbon brand Wild Turkey as well as its namesake bitter liqueur.

Apple Supplier Luxshare to Maintain Vietnam Production Hub to Support U.S. Market

Luxshare Precision Industry, a key assembler of the iPhone and other Apple products, said it will maintain large-scale production in Vietnam and continue exports from there to the U.S., responding to President Trump's tariffs.

Bain Capital to Take 9.9% Stake in Lincoln National

The two parties are set to enter into a 10-year, nonexclusive strategic investment-management relationship.

Post Holdings to Close Two Cereal Plants, Cut 300 Jobs

Post Holdings plans to close cereal manufacturing plants in Ontario and Nevada, putting about 300 employees out of work, as demand in the ready-to-eat cereal category continues to decline.

data.title: Auteur Wines Welcomes Guests to New Russian River Home
data.description:
Healdsburg, CA, April 09, 2025 (GLOBE NEWSWIRE) — The culmination of a deeply held vision, co-owners Kenneth and Laura Juhasz are honored to announce the opening of Auteur Russian River, a striking new visitor destination on Wohler Road outside of Healdsburg. Perched on a wooded vineyard hillside, this intimate retreat embraces the region’s vineyards and coastal forests, offering immersive hospitality experiences that draw guests into a setting as layered and expressive as Auteur’s wines.

“We immediately felt a powerful affinity for this piece of land – with its rugged slope of bay, redwood and fir trees, sweeping vineyard views, and the natural spring which has nourished the flora, fauna and human visitors for centuries,” said Kenneth Juhasz, Auteur co-founder and winemaker. “Wine bears witness to the cycles of nature, and we are humbled to begin marking each season from our new home here in the Russian River Valley, a pillar of world-class viticulture. Auteur Russian River is a place of renewal where guests can connect, reflect, and simply be.” 

Auteur is known for Pinot Noir and Chardonnay and has quietly built a passionate following since its founding in 2003. Unwavering in the pursuit of bold choices and fearless winemaking, the vintner-founded team is independent to explore and fully commit to the creative process, surrendering to the land and lifecycle of each vintage. 

"Our vision has always been to create wines that capture a sense of time and place," continued Laura Juhasz, Auteur co-founder. "With this new home, we have an opportunity to extend that philosophy beyond the glass – crafting an experience that invites guests to slow down, connect, and savor the moment. This project would not have been possible without the collaboration and dedication of so many over the past 20-plus years, and Kenneth and I feel incredibly fortunate.”

A Space Rooted in Place & Possibility

Blending modern aesthetics with agrarian inspiration, the new Auteur destination, designed by Bohlin Cywinski Jackson and with construction by Earthtone Construction, feels deeply connected to the terrain. Natural materials, warm wood paneling, and panoramic views set the tone for a welcoming environment. Floor-to-ceiling windows frame uninterrupted views, while a redwood and blackened steel hearth anchors the main room, creating an atmosphere of refined comfort. Large sliding doors open onto a covered terrace, where visitors can enjoy their wines amidst the rolling landscape. 

“Designing this place so that it feels connected to its extraordinary setting was our core goal,” said architect Gregory Mottola, FAIA, principal at Bohlin Cywinski Jackson. “We used the steepness of the site to help guests feel nestled at the edge of the hillside, as if floating above the valley. Natural elements are woven into every part of the design, while expansive windows, one of the most striking features, connect guests to sweeping views while capturing the drama of changing light throughout the day – it is truly magical.”

The outdoor environment, designed by landscape architect James Munden of Munden Fry Landscape Associates, is emphasized by native insectary plantings, which support biodiversity. Ornamental, drought-tolerant grasses begin at the entry gate and flow toward the tasting terrace, expressing a wind-swept coastal influence. The planting design wraps the building and softens the edges of the vineyard while interweaving amongst a network of bioswales, reducing erosion and helping to retain water on site. Landscape contractor Lucas Dexter of DEXTER estate landscapes executed the design on-site, bringing the overall vision to life.

Thoughtfully Curated Guest Experiences

Wine and culinary tasting offerings spark discovery and deepen guests' appreciation for the nuances of site and vintage. Signature tastings highlight limited-production, terroir-driven Chardonnay and Pinot Noir, while immersive sensory experiences invite guests to explore the intersections of wine, conviviality and nature. Chef Elizabeth Payne, who has lived and worked in the Bay Area and Sonoma County for decades, will offer a seasonal menu of small bites and pairings that highlight locally sourced ingredients. Select experiences include: 

•    Be Here Now - A sensory-driven experience where guests taste wines without prior knowledge, allowing for an unbiased and introspective discovery.

•    Musing of Clones - Sharpen the lens on the history and specific qualities of Chardonnay and Pinot Noir clones and how they differ from region to region.

•    From The Archives - A retrospective library tasting showcasing the historical years of Auteur wines, each pour telling a story of a moment in time.

A Commitment to Community & Creativity

Beyond offering exceptional wines, Auteur is dedicated to fostering a thriving community. During the opening period, the winery is partnering with Farm to Pantry, a BIPOC/women-run, Sonoma County-based organization that gleans produce that would otherwise go wasted and shares it with those facing food insecurity. Ongoing, the winery will host collaborations with local authors, artisans, and nonprofit organizations, ensuring Auteur Russian River is a place not just for wine appreciation, but for meaningful connection and shared experiences.

Auteur Russian River’s grand opening, featuring special wines, pairings and more, will take place April 11, 2025. 

Location: 10520 Wohler Rd, Healdsburg, CA 95448

Hours: Daily, 10:00 a.m. - 5:00 p.m.

Reservations Recommended: Reservations@auteurwines.com

Auteur will also continue to host guests in the town of Sonoma at their Bungalow on First Street West. 

About Auteur Wines

Founded in 2003 by Kenneth and Laura Juhasz, Auteur creates wines of intention, elegance, and authenticity. Its exceptional, small-lot Pinot Noir and Chardonnay are sourced from sustainable vineyards in distinct micro-terroirs along the Northern California Coast. Each wine transposes place and time to tell its own story, reflecting a deep respect for nature and a commitment to craftsmanship. Learn more at auteurwines.com or follow @auteurwines on social media.  

# # #

Attachments

  • Auteur Russian River Estate
  • A Space Rooted in Place & Possibility

Ashley Teplin

Studio 707 for Auteur Wines

310.210.3067

ashley@studio-707.com

Aimee Sands Carney

Studio 707 for Auteur Wines

707.363.0179

aimee@studio-707.com

data.title: Trump administration backs off Nvidia's H20 chip crackdown after Mar-a-Lago dinner, NPR reports
data.description:
The Trump administration has reversed course on plans to restrict exports of Nvidia's H20 artificial intelligence chips to China after CEO Jensen Huang attended a Mar-a-Lago dinner last week, NPR reported on Wednesday.

The planned American export controls on the chips had been in the works for months, NPR reported, citing two sources, and were ready to be implemented as soon as this week.

The change in plans came after Nvidia promised the Trump administration new U.S. investments in AI data centers, the NPR report said.

The White House and Nvidia did not immediately respond to Reuters' requests for comment.

U.S. President Donald Trump's administration was considering tightening restrictions on the AI leader's sales of its H20 chips designed for the China market, Reuters had reported in January.

data.title: US 10-Year Auction High Yield Rises From Previous Auction, Demand Higher
data.description:
The US Treasury's 10-year auction hit a high yield of 4.435% on Wednesday, up from the 4.310% high in the previous auction.

The bid to cover ratio for the auction was 2.67, above the 2.59 ratio in the previous auction.

Dealers represented 51.02% of the bids, with direct bidders at 7.50% and indirect bidders at 41.48%.

For takedown, dealers took 10.70%, with direct bidders at 1.40% and indirect bidders at 87.90%.

data.title: National Bank Monthly Canadian Freight Update
data.description:
The Cass Transportation Shipments Index, which tracks overall freight demand in North America, was down 5.5% in February, while the Expenditures Index was down 4.6%, notes National Bank in its

Monthly Canadian Freight Update.

Highlights:

Combined Canadian rail volumes were up 2.4% in the first quarter, with revenue ton miles (RTMs) for CN was up 1.4% and up 3.6% for CPKC.

Combined Intermodal RTMs were up 2.3% in the quarter with CN up 1.4% and CPKC up 3.6%.

Combined TEU (20-foot equivalent) volumes at Canada's major ports were down 15.4% in February as volumes were impacted by weather and higher than normal dwell times at the ports, National Bank notes.

Global air cargo tonne-kilometers were down 0.1% in February, the first y/y decline since July 2023 while available capacity was down 0.4%.

data.title: Nymex Overview: WTI Hits Four-Year Low as Trade War, Demand Fears MountOPIS
data.description:
The West Texas Intermediate crude contract fell more than $2 by midday Wednesday, as intensifying fears of a global trade war and lower petroleum demand expectations sent the U.S. oil benchmark to its lowest level in more than four years.

At noon ET, May WTI fell $2.30 to $57.30/bbl, on track for a fifth session of sharp declines and to settle at its lowest since Feb. 5, 2021. June WTI was about $2.25 lower at $56.85/bbl.

WTI has lost around $14, or 20%, since its April 2 settlement of $71.71/bbl.

London-based June Brent crude was off by $2.30 to $60.55/bbl and July Brent fell by a similar amount to $60.05/bbl.

Refined product futures tracked crude oil's sharp decline. May RBOB was 9.4cts lower at $1.8975/gal and June RBOB slid 9cts to $1.89/gal. May ULSD fell 8.15cts to $1.9755/gal and June ULSD was down by 6.95cts to $1.9355/gal.

President Trump's reciprocal tariffs on about 100 nations took effect overnight, including a 104% tariff on Chinese imports. On Wednesday, Beijing said it would increase levies on U.S. imports to 84%, up from 34%.

Most economists believe a trade war is detrimental to global economic growth as it typically forces countries to produce and consume products at a higher cost.

The prospect of a global recession has weighed heavily on world petroleum demand expectations.

In crude supply news, the Keystone Pipeline, a major crude oil supplier to U.S. refineries, remained shut following a Tuesday morning spill in southeastern North Dakota. The pipeline's operator estimated the release volume was about 3,500 bbl. The Keystone Pipeline system delivers Canadian and U.S. crude oil supplies to refiners in Illinois, Oklahoma and Texas.

This content was created by Oil Price Information Service, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. OPIS is run independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

  • Reporting by Frank Tang, ftang@opisnet.com; Editing by Michael Kelly, mkelly@opisnet.com

data.title: Invesco Cut to Hold From Buy by TD Cowen
Ag Market Resists Tariff VolatilityMarket Talk
data.description: Agricultural futures have been a mixed bag, with some under pressure while others remain positive or neutral. Corn and wheat are both down 0.4%, but soybeans have managed to stay flat--this despite China's importance to the soybean export sales market. But grain traders are not wrapped up in fear-driven trading, says Virginia McGathey of McGathey Commodities. "While the uncertainty is holding grains near the lows, there isn't that panic selling to wash out markets," says McGathey. She also adds that tomorrow's WASDE report has traders thinking more about fundamentals ahead of spring planting than gyrations in world trade. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

data.title: US 10-Year Treasury Sells Off
data.description:
The yield on the US 10-year Treasury note rose as much as 20bps to 4.5% on Wednesday before easing to 4.4% after a stronger-than-expected auction for 10-year notes offset lower demand by primary dealers earlier this month.

Still, the ongoing selloff in bonds reflected mounting investor anxiety following Trump’s reciprocal tariffs, a concern intensified by China’s latest move to impose an additional 50% retaliatory tariff and the EU’s approval of tariffs targeting €21 billion worth of US goods.

There were reports of foreign selling and signs of a broader flight to cash, as some investors appeared to be liquidating positions - adding to concerns that US bonds may be losing their traditional safe-haven appeal.

Japan, China, and the UK, the biggest holders of US Treasuries and likely auction participants—are also among the nations hit hardest by US tariffs.

The tariff escalation has fueled recession fears and inflation concerns, potentially limiting the Fed’s ability to ease policy.

data.title: Walmart: Tariffs Increase Uncertainty on Profit Growth This Quarter
data.description:
By Mackenzie Tatananni and Sabrina Escobar

Walmart warned there is more uncertainty about how fast its profits will grow in the first quarter, citing President Donald Trump's latest barrage of tariffs.

The retailer said Wednesday that the possible range for its adjusted operating income growth has widened for the first quarter, a shift from its guidance for year-over-year growth between 0.5% and 2% provided Feb. 20. It didn't provide a new range.

"The range of outcomes... has widened due to less favorable category mix, higher casualty claims expense and the desire to maintain flexibility to invest in price as tariffs are implemented," Walmart said in a news release Wednesday, ahead of the company's biennial analyst day.

Walmart reaffirmed its sales guidance for the quarter and noted that its fiscal 2026 outlook remained unchanged despite seeing higher volatility in demand in recent weeks. It expects first-quarter net sales to increase between 3% and 4% from the year-ago quarter. Full-year net sales will also grow from 3% to 4%, with an adjusted operating income increase of 3.5% to 5.5%.

"We view the update as better than feared vs. our expectation of the company potentially withdrawing full-year guidance given recent tariff developments," wrote Rupesh Parikh, an analyst at Oppenheimer.

Walmart stock was up 4% to $85.04 in early morning trading Wednesday, reversing premarket losses. Shares are down 6.5% this year.

As the world's largest retailer, Walmart's path forward in a higher-tariff environment will clue investors in on how other businesses may proceed.

Imported goods accounted for roughly a third of Walmart's U.S. sales last year. The majority are sourced from China, Mexico, and Vietnam, three countries that face so-called reciprocal tariffs. The sweeping levies, including a 104% tax on Chinese imports, went into effect Wednesday.

Executives said the company was "still working through" what the new tariffs mean for Walmart, but hope to give investors more insight into how the tariffs would influence the business in the short term later in the day.

"I've seen us navigate times like the period after 9/11, the global financial crisis, the pandemic, and, more recently, high inflation," said CEO Doug McMillon. "While in the short term, we're not immune to some of the effects, we are positioned to play offense. Nothing about the current environment impacts our confidence in our business or strategy."

There are bright spots despite the tumultuous macro backdrop, which was helping drive shares higher Wednesday. Citi Research analysts noted Tuesday that Walmart takes market share from competitors during periods of inflation. In spite of its reputation as a major importer, Walmart relies more on domestic sourcing than most businesses driven by consumables, the analysts added.

The company is also continuing to reap the benefits of its investments in e-commerce and alternative revenue sources.

Walmart's e-commerce business in the U.S. turned profitable for the first time ever this quarter, and the company expects the full year will be profitable as well, said John David Rainey, Walmart's chief financial officer.

Meanwhile, its new businesses, including advertising and fintech, have grown at a 40% annual rate over the last four years. Executives said "that's a good way to think about the growth in these areas" over the next five years. Two-thirds of profit growth over the next several years will come from these digital and high-growth businesses, Rainey said.

"The fastest-growing parts of our business also have the highest incremental margins," Rainey said.

Write to Mackenzie Tatananni at mackenzie.tatananni@barrons.com and Sabrina Escobar at sabrina.escobar@barrons.com

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: Treasury Yields Pare Gains After 10-Year Note Auction
data.description:
By Sam Goldfarb

Bond prices are rebounding after a much-anticipated $39 billion government auction of 10-year U.S. Treasury notes met with strong demand from investors.

Treasury yields, which rise when bond prices fall, have surged this week, raising fears among investors that a bad auction would intensify the selloff.

Investors have been concerned that foreign demand for Treasurys could shrink in response to President Trump's aggressive tariff actions.

This item is part of a Wall Street Journal live coverage event. The full stream can be found by searching P/WSJL (WSJ Live Coverage).

data.title: Cal-Maine Profit Soars on High Egg Prices. A Justice Department Probe Casts a Shadow.
data.description:
By Evie Liu

Cal-Maine Foods stock fell as much as 5% on Wednesday morning after quarterly earnings missed estimates despite strong results, and the nation's largest egg producer said it's cooperating with a government investigation into price gouging.

The stock recovered in afternoon trading, up 0.94% to $91.18.

Egg prices surged in recent months as the avian-flu outbreak killed millions of egg-laying chickens across the nation. Cal-Maine said on Tuesday that its average selling price of a dozen eggs was $4.06 in the fiscal third quarter ended on March 1, nearly double the $2.25 mark from a year ago.

Still, the company said it sold a record number of eggs during the quarter due to "strong consumer demand." Cal-Maine said its large operational scale has helped it increase production capacity despite the challenging environment, noting that the average number of its layer and breeder flocks both went up during the quarter from a year ago.

In the third quarter, Cal-Maine's revenue more than doubled from a year ago to $1.42 billion, while earnings per share reached $10.38, up 246% from the previous year. But the results were short of Wall Street's estimates. Analysts polled by FactSet had expected the egg producer to earn $10.72 a share in earnings on $1.43 billion in sales.

Investors have more to worry about. The Justice Department recently launched an investigation into whether egg producers violated antitrust laws to boost egg prices higher than what they should be — or the so-called price gouging. Cal-Maine said it received a request for information last month and was cooperating.

Cal-Maine's board approved $500 million worth of share repurchase during the third quarter and declared a cash dividend of approximately $3.46 per share.

The company also announced an agreement to acquire Echo Lake Foods, which makes breakfast foods like scrambled eggs and frozen omelets, for around $258 million. Cal-Maine said the deal would boost its presence in the prepared food business.

Egg prices have come down lately as the bird flu alleviates and consumers cut their purchases, but the numbers can tick up anytime if the virus starts spreading again. Wall Street expects Cal-Maine's earnings for fiscal 2025, which ends in May, to be $24.45 per share, more than four times the profits in the last fiscal year.

Last month, President Donald Trump's administration announced a $1 billion plan to fight avian flu and bring egg prices down, which includes enhancing biosecurity measures on farms and plans to import more eggs from abroad. But the imports could run up against Trump's latest round of tariffs that target a range of trade partners.

Write to Evie Liu at evie.liu@barrons.com

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: Analysts See U.S. Natural Gas Storage Up By 52 Billion-Cubic-Feet
data.description:
By Anthony Harrup

U.S. natural gas inventories likely rose by more than usual last week, further reducing the deficit against the five-year average and year-ago levels, according to a survey by The Wall Street Journal.

Gas in underground storage is expected to have increased by 52 billion cubic feet to 1,825 Bcf in the week ended April 4, according to the average estimate of 11 analysts, brokers and traders. Forecasts range from an injection of 43 Bcf to an injection of 63 Bcf.

The expected increase is larger than the 17 Bcf five-year average injection for the week, and would reduce the deficit to 45 Bcf from 80 Bcf the week before. It would leave storage 455 Bcf below the year-earlier level.

A colder-than-normal January and February this year led inventories to shift from a surplus over the five-year average to a deficit in excess of 200 Bcf, but the injection season got off to an early start with mild March weather, significantly reducing the gap.

The U.S. Energy Information Administration is scheduled to release weekly storage data on Thursday at 10:30 a.m. EDT.

Write to Anthony Harrup at anthony.harrup@wsj.com

Tradeweb Announces Date for First Quarter 2025 Financial Results

NEW YORK--(BUSINESS WIRE)--April 09, 2025--

Tradeweb Markets Inc. , a leading, global operator of electronic marketplaces for rates, credit, equities and money markets, will release financial results for the first quarter of 2025 on Wednesday, April 30, 2025, at approximately 7:00 AM EDT.

In addition, Tradeweb will host a conference call for investors.

A live webcast of the conference call, along with related presentation materials, will be available at https://investors.tradeweb.com/events-and-presentations.

About Tradeweb Markets

Tradeweb Markets Inc. is a leading, global operator of electronic marketplaces for rates, credit, equities and money markets. Founded in 1996, Tradeweb provides access to markets, data and analytics, electronic trading, straight-through-processing and reporting for more than 50 products to clients in the institutional, wholesale, retail and corporates markets. Advanced technologies developed by Tradeweb enhance price discovery, order execution and trade workflows while allowing for greater scale and helping to reduce risks in client trading operations. Tradeweb serves more than 3,000 clients in more than 85 countries. On average, Tradeweb facilitated more than $2.2 trillion in notional value traded per day over the past four fiscal quarters. For more information, please go to www.tradeweb.com.

data.title: Restaurants Canada is extremely pleased by new loan program and alcohol discount announced in Newfoundland and Labrador’s budget
data.description:
St. John's, April 09, 2025 (GLOBE NEWSWIRE) — Restaurants Canada is very pleased by today’s announcement from the government of Newfoundland and Labrador of a $10 million restaurant loan guarantee program and doubling the alcohol wholesale discounts to 10% for licensed restaurants on wine, spirits and ready-to-drink beverages.

These measures will help the province’s more than 1,000 foodservice businesses weather economic pressures, protect jobs and keep food prices affordable.

Alcohol is a critical part of a restaurant’s revenue, so lowering the wholesale cost is a very effective measure to reduce the cost of doing business and strengthen profitability. Those sales also translate into more tax paid to the province, offsetting the cost of the discount.

The pandemic and its aftershocks have taken a toll on Newfoundland and Labrador’s restaurant industry. There are 2,600 fewer foodservice jobs in the province today than there were in 2019 and a slew of closures of high-profile restaurants has rocked the industry recently. The restaurant loan guarantee program will provide a lifeline to struggling businesses and give them the time they need to adjust to current market conditions.

Restaurants Canada continues to call for measures that will support the foodservice industry, such as exempting all food from GST/HST and reducing interprovincial trade barriers. Today’s announcement from the Newfoundland and Labrador government shows that it is listening.

  • Janick Cormier, Vice-President for Atlantic Canada, Restaurants Canada

About Restaurants Canada

Restaurants Canada is a national, not-for-profit association advancing Canada’s diverse and dynamic foodservice industry. Restaurants are a nearly $120 billion industry employing 1.2 million Canadians and is the number one source of first-time jobs in Canada.

Milena Stanoeva

Restaurants Canada

6479211758

media@restaurantscanada.org
